text: The surgeon places the first two laparoscopic ports and performs an extensive lysis of adhesions, using the camera and working instrument to navigate and separate the adhesions effectively.

The surgeon places the first two laparoscopic ports and performs extensive lysis of adhesions within the abdominal cavity, using the camera and working instrument to navigate and manipulate the tissue.
The surgeon maneuvers instruments in the hypogastrium to create space for the placement of the third and fourth robotic trocars.
The surgeon manipulates tissue in the hypogastrium to create space for the placement of the third and fourth robotic trocars.
The surgeon exposes the midline, revealing previously placed proline sutures. Space is created for the placement of the third robotic arm, and the lysis of adhesions continues in the surgical field.

The surgeon exposes the midline, revealing previously placed proline sutures. Space is created for the placement of robotic arm number three, and the lysis of adhesions continues in the surgical field.
The surgeon adjusts the surgical field to create space for the placement of the fourth robotic arm, ensuring adequate room for its operation.

The surgeon places a twelve millimeter trocar in the left upper quadrant and begins robotic lysis of adhesions to fully expose the abdomen. The midline shows multiple hernia defects resembling Swiss cheese. The surgeon avoids the midline for closure, opting to exteriorize through a right upper quadrant trocar site and uses a zero V lock suture to placate the midline without mesh.

A twelve millimeter trocar is placed in the left upper quadrant at the original trocar position. The surgeon begins robotic lysis of adhesions to fully expose the abdomen, revealing a Swiss cheese appearance of the midline. The midline is avoided for closure, opting instead to exteriorize through a right upper quadrant trocar site. The midline is placated upon closure using a zero V lock suture to close the Swiss cheese hernia defects without mesh.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to enter the gastrohepatic ligament, exposing the transverse colon tumor. The dissection progresses above the transverse colon into the lesser sac.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to enter the gastrohepatic ligament, exposing the transverse colon tumor. The procedure involves dissecting the omentum from the transverse colon to fully reveal the tumor, while some omentum remains adherent in the pelvis.

The surgeon uses a surgical instrument to detach the omentum from the transverse colon, aiming to fully expose the tumor. Some omentum remains adherent in the pelvis, which is being amputated at this stage.

The surgeon has exposed the tumor on the transverse colon, performing a mesenteric dissection by closely following the bowel wall. The tumor appears obstructive, and a palliative resection is being conducted due to the patient's medical comorbidities and previous surgical history.

The surgeon has exposed the transverse colon tumor and is performing a mesenteric dissection by closely following the bowel wall. The vessel sealer is used effectively to dissect around the tumor, which is visibly obstructing the colon.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers the vessel sealer underneath the bowel wall, maintaining close proximity to the tissue during the dissection.

The surgeon creates a window through the mesentery, continuing to separate it from the specimen. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to confirm the intact blood supply to the colon, which appears satisfactory. The surgeon then uses the right upper quadrant trocar to divide the mesentery proximally and distally.

The surgeon creates a window through the mesentery, taking the remaining mesentery to the specimen. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to confirm the blood supply to the colon, which appears intact. The surgeon then uses the right upper quadrant trocar to divide the tissue proximally and distally.

The surgeon aligns an isoparastaltic anastomosis and creates enterotomies using scissors. A white load stapler is used to form a common channel in the right upper quadrant. The common enterotomy is closed with a second layer of sutures, completing the procedure.

The surgeon aligns an isoparastaltic anastomosis and makes enterotomies using scissors. A white load stapler is used to form a common channel in the right upper quadrant. The surgeon then closes the common enterotomy with a second layer of sutures.
The surgeon covers the surgical area with omentum and placates the midline using a zero V-lock suture.
